metadata.file_name: 92. Park et al. (2025). Measurement Invariance for Multilingual Learners Using Item Response and Response Time in PISA 2018.pdf
metadata.title: Measurement Invariance for Multilingual Learners Using Item Response and Response Time in PISA 2018

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PISA 2018 item responses and item-level response times for a U.S. sample of 15-year-old students, with an analytic split into multilingual learners (MLs) versus non-MLs based on home language. The authors describe the measurement-invariance workflow using multigroup IRT (2PL for dichotomous items and GPCM for polytomous items), DIF detection via RMSD thresholds, and response-time invariance via LOWESS and the Guo & Ercikan differential response time (DRT) test. The manuscript does not report applying PISA sampling weights (e.g., W_FSTUWT/TOTWGT) or replicate-weight methods (e.g., BRR/jackknife), and the analysis is conducted with IRT/WLE and DIF/DRT procedures rather than ML models that would typically require explicit weight handling. Therefore, weighting is treated as not applied/unspecified in the reported methodology.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: The paper does not use PISA plausible values (PVs) for achievement outcomes; instead, it estimates latent ability (theta) using weighted likelihood estimates (WLE) within a multigroup IRT framework and then evaluates measurement invariance (DIF) and response-time invariance (DRT). Because the dependent constructs are IRT-based latent traits and response-time comparisons rather than PV-based achievement scores, PV handling is not applicable. The manuscript also does not describe a missing-data strategy (e.g., complete-case rules or imputation), representing a reporting gap for how missing item responses/response times were treated prior to IRT estimation and DRT testing.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used the PISA 2018 U.S. student sample (15-year-olds) and classified students as MLs vs non-MLs using the questionnaire item 'What language do you speak at home most of the time' (home language = language of the test → non-ML; 'Other languages' → ML). The study analyzed all available items within reading, mathematics, and science domains generated from a design-based assessment (DBA), totaling 511 items for scored responses (366 reading, 70 mathematics, 115 science). For response-time analyses, it used all available item-level response-time data for 423 items (238 reading, 70 mathematics, 115 science), with response-time variables identified by names ending in 'TT'. No additional exclusion criteria (e.g., complete-case rules for missing responses/RTs) are explicitly stated.

main_findings.target_variable: Differential item functioning (DIF) in scored responses
main_findings.performance_metrics: DIF detected using multigroup IRT RMSD thresholding (RMSD > .12). In reading, 13.66% of items (50 items) showed DIF for MLs; 12.81% of computer-scored items (36/281) and 16.47% of human-coded items (14/85).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Reading data for U.S. students, the study compared item functioning between home language groups (ML vs non-ML) to assess DIF in scored responses, finding that a larger share of reading items exhibited DIF for MLs (13.66% overall), indicating measurement non-comparability even after controlling for latent ability via multigroup IRT. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Latent ability differences (WLE theta) between ML and non-ML
main_findings.performance_metrics: After releasing DIF-identified item parameter constraints, non-MLs had higher latent ability than MLs: t(4,778) = 6.51, p < .001, d = .26 (small effect).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Reading data for U.S. students, the study leveraged multigroup IRT WLE latent ability estimates to compare ML and non-ML groups, finding that non-MLs had significantly higher reading ability than MLs (t(4,778)=6.51, d=0.26). This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Differential response time (DRT) between ML and non-ML
main_findings.performance_metrics: DRT test (Guo & Ercikan) exceeded critical value 1.96 for 18 items (8%) in reading. DRT effect size range: (–8.36, 42.59). Positive DRT indicates MLs spent more time than non-MLs; overall MLs generally had slower response times when abilities were matched.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Reading data for U.S. students, the study compared response-time functioning between ML and non-ML groups using DRT, finding that 8% of reading items showed significant DRT and that MLs generally spent more time than non-MLs at matched ability levels. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Differential item functioning (DIF) in scored responses
main_findings.performance_metrics: In mathematics, 7.14% of items (5 items) showed DIF for MLs; 5.77% of computer-scored items (3/52) and 11.11% of human-coded items (2/18).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Mathematics data for U.S. students, the study compared item functioning between ML and non-ML groups to assess DIF in scored responses, finding that DIF was less prevalent in mathematics than in reading (7.14% of items overall), with DIF concentrated in the ML group. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Latent ability differences (WLE theta) between ML and non-ML
main_findings.performance_metrics: Non-MLs had higher latent ability than MLs: t(2,594) = 4.19, p < .001, d = .23 (small effect). After controlling for reading ability via ANCOVA, ML and non-ML differences in mathematics were no longer significant.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Mathematics data for U.S. students, the study compared ML and non-ML groups using multigroup IRT WLE latent ability estimates, finding that non-MLs initially scored higher (t(2,594)=4.19, d=0.23) but that the group difference in mathematics disappeared after controlling for reading ability. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Differential response time (DRT) between ML and non-ML
main_findings.performance_metrics: DRT test exceeded critical value 1.96 for 7 items (10%) in mathematics. DRT effect size range: (–12.61, 42.68). Positive DRT values were most common in mathematics (89%), indicating MLs were predominantly slower.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Mathematics data for U.S. students, the study compared response-time functioning between ML and non-ML groups using DRT, finding that 10% of mathematics items showed significant DRT and that DRT effects were predominantly positive (MLs slower). This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Differential item functioning (DIF) in scored responses
main_findings.performance_metrics: In science, 11% of items (13 items) showed DIF for MLs; 13.25% of computer-scored items (11/83) and 6.25% of human-coded items (2/32).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Science data for U.S. students, the study compared item functioning between ML and non-ML groups to assess DIF in scored responses, finding that 11% of science items exhibited DIF for MLs, again suggesting group-specific item behavior. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Latent ability differences (WLE theta) between ML and non-ML
main_findings.performance_metrics: Non-MLs had higher latent ability than MLs: t(2,588) = 7.18, p < .001, d = .39 (small effect). After controlling for reading ability via ANCOVA, the ML–non-ML difference in science remained significant (F(1, 2,578) = 18.53, p < .001).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Science data for U.S. students, the study compared ML and non-ML groups using multigroup IRT WLE latent ability estimates, finding that non-MLs had higher science ability (t(2,588)=7.18, d=0.39) and that the disparity persisted even after controlling for reading ability. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.target_variable: Differential response time (DRT) between ML and non-ML
main_findings.performance_metrics: DRT test exceeded critical value 1.96 for 8 items (7%) in science. DRT effect size range: (–6.82, 41.52). Positive DRT values were most common in science (85%), indicating MLs were predominantly slower.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2018 Science data for U.S. students, the study compared response-time functioning between ML and non-ML groups using DRT, finding that 7% of science items showed significant DRT and that MLs generally spent more time than non-MLs at matched ability levels. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: This study uses PISA 2018 U.S. data to examine assessment bias for multilingual learners (MLs) versus non-MLs using multigroup IRT DIF (RMSD thresholds) on scored responses and invariance of item response times via LOWESS and the Guo & Ercikan DRT test. Across reading, mathematics, and science, DIF was present for a minority of items (about 14% in reading, 7% in mathematics, and 11% in science), indicating that some items function differently for MLs even after accounting for latent ability. Latent ability estimates (WLE theta) showed non-MLs scoring higher in all three domains; however, the mathematics gap disappeared after controlling for reading ability, while the science gap remained significant. Response-time analyses consistently indicated that MLs spent more time than non-MLs when abilities were matched, with DRT significant for 8% (reading), 10% (math), and 7% (science) of items. The paper does not report a missing-data strategy and does not apply/describe PISA sampling weights, which may affect generalizability.
